narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, based on academic and background factors from past students. The model estimated an 88.3% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which is high enough to trigger an automatic rejection of your admission to protect the school's outcomes. This decision hinged primarily on your early academic performance in school, where your Year 3 grades placed you in the bottom decile—ranking 1 out of 10 compared to the typical student's middle-of-the-pack position around the 5th or 6th decile. That low Year 3 standing was the single biggest factor pushing the prediction toward failure, as it signals potential challenges in handling rigorous legal coursework ahead. Your Year 1 grades, also in the bottom decile versus the average around the 6th, reinforced this concern as the second-most influential element, suggesting a consistent early struggle that the model views as a red flag for bar success. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA of 2.90—slightly below the typical 3.21 average—actually nudged the prediction a bit toward passing, showing some resilience in college-level work. Other details like your middle-family income bracket, LSAT score of 29 (well below the average 37), and full-time student status didn't sway the top decision as much, though they're part of the overall picture. Compared to typical admitted students, who often enter with stronger high school deciles and higher test scores, your profile stood out for those early academic hurdles that historically correlate with bar struggles. While your background as an Asian male doesn't directly factor in, the model focuses purely on these performance indicators shared across all applicants. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your recent academic record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and gathering strong recommendation letters that highlight your growth potential. Many students in similar positions have succeeded by addressing these gaps—reapplying after targeted improvements could shift your prediction dramatically. You're clearly motivated to pursue law, and with focused effort, you can rewrite this story.
